data.title: AI marketing upstart submits its IPO application: Titanium Technology earns 380 million yuan annually but has 3.8 billion yuan in accounts receivable.
data.description:
Author: Sun Cheng; Source: Barron's

Recently, AI marketing company Titanium Technology filed for an IPO on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, aiming to become the "first multi-agent stock."

Against the backdrop of widespread losses in the AI ​​industry, this company delivered a rare profit report: a gross profit margin exceeding 82% and a net revenue retention rate as high as 132.5%. However, behind these impressive figures, high accounts receivable exceeding $557 million, heavy reliance on top media outlets, and a continuous decline in gross profit margin are subjecting this "AI marketing dark horse" to rigorous market scrutiny.

Strong Profitability, but High Accounts Receivable Present Significant Concerns

From a financial perspective, Titanium Technology exhibits extremely rare profitability.

The prospectus shows that the company's revenue was approximately US$72.82 million, US$102 million, and US$130 million in 2023, 2024, and the first nine months of 2025, respectively; and its profit was US$34.345 million, US$50.999 million, and US$55.679 million, respectively. More noteworthy is its profit margin; the gross profit margin remained stable above 82% during the reporting period, and the net profit margin once approached 50%. Although it fell back to 43.0% in the first nine months of 2025, it is still at a high level within the industry. The company's main business has strong cash-generating capabilities, with continuous net inflows of operating cash flow, and cash reserves rapidly accumulated from US$197 million at the end of 2023 to US$441 million at the end of September 2025. However, behind these impressive profit figures lie hidden risks. At the end of each reporting period, the company's net trade and other receivables increased year by year, reaching approximately US$254 million, US$402 million, and US$557 million, respectively. As of the end of September 2025, this figure was four times the annual revenue for the same period, and the potential risk of bad debts cannot be ignored. Furthermore, the company's gross profit margin has shown a continuous downward trend, decreasing from 84.6% to 82.2%. Overall, Titanium Technology is an AI marketing company with strong monetization and cash flow generation capabilities, but the quality of its balance sheet and the sustainability of its profitability still need to withstand longer market scrutiny. R&D expenses surged by 137%, accounts receivable are high, and cash flow is under pressure. Titanium Technology's core competitiveness lies in its technology roadmap of focusing on vertical scenarios. Unlike general-purpose large models that rely on static, non-commercial data for training, the company's self-developed "Titanium Extreme" multimodal large model is specifically designed for cross-border marketing scenarios, deeply integrating global market dynamics and real business feedback. This hybrid expert (MoE) architecture, which integrates "AI+BI," enables it to automatically calibrate strategies based on actual campaign performance, achieving continuous evolution that "gets stronger with use." Launched in October 2025 based on the Titanium Maximization Model, Navos, one of the world's first marketing multi-agent systems, has transformed technological advantages into commercial value. According to feedback from some core clients, a complete marketing cycle that previously took 1 to 3 months has been shortened to within hours after Navos' intervention. This ability to compress the marketing cycle from "months" to "hours" is Titanium Technology's core weapon for attracting advertisers and maintaining high stickiness. This technological advantage is directly reflected in client data. As of 2025, Titanium Technology has served over 100,000 advertisers, connecting with 7 million media platforms globally, with a top client coverage rate exceeding 80%. Its client list includes well-known companies such as Alibaba, ByteDance, Skechers, and 37Games. Notably, the company's net revenue retention rate reached 132.5%, indicating that the scale of cooperation with existing clients continues to expand. Based on 2024 revenue, Frost & Sullivan ranked it as the number one Chinese domestic AI marketing technology service provider going global, with a market share of 8.5%. However, Titanium Technology's weaknesses are just as pronounced as its strengths. First, there's the persistently high risk of accounts receivable. The net amount of $557 million in trade and other receivables poses a significant risk. For a light-asset AI marketing company, cash flow difficulties could have a cascading impact on its operations. Although the company explains that this stems from business expansion and granting credit terms to high-quality clients, and that the turnover days of trade receivables were largely manageable during the reporting period, the continued increase in absolute amounts remains a Damocles' sword hanging over its cash flow. Second, there's the high dependence of its business model. The company's business is heavily reliant on partnerships with leading global media platforms such as Meta, Google, and TikTok. The prospectus frankly states that "most media resources come from a few media partners." This concentration risk means that if these platforms adjust their cooperation policies, increase procurement costs, or even terminate cooperation, it will have a significant impact on the company's business. Although the company attempts to build barriers through technological value, it still struggles to gain absolute bargaining power against these giants. Furthermore, profitability indicators have shown signs of weakness. Gross profit margin declined from 84.6% to 82.2%, which the company explained was due to increased revenue contribution from customized influencer marketing solutions, which have lower gross margins. Meanwhile, R&D expenditure is rapidly increasing, rising 137% year-on-year to US$16.7 million in the first nine months of 2025, accounting for 12.8% of revenue. Against the backdrop of intensifying competition in the AI ​​marketing field, continuous "cash-burning" R&D is a necessary cost to maintain technological leadership, but it also puts continuous pressure on profits. The blue ocean market and the presence of giants: The overseas AI marketing market in which Titanium Technology operates has immense potential. According to Frost & Sullivan data, the global marketing technology market size is projected to grow from US$25.9 billion in 2024 to US$118.2 billion in 2029, with a CAGR of 35.5%. This growth is mainly driven by increased global e-commerce penetration and enterprises shifting their digital marketing budgets towards AI. Among them, the Chinese AI marketing technology market going global is expected to grow from $1.1 billion in 2024 to $6.4 billion in 2029, with a compound annual growth rate of 42.2%, becoming a core growth driver in the globalization wave of Chinese enterprises. However, this blue ocean market is never short of competitors. Just one day before Titanium Technology submitted its application, A-share listed company Yidian Tianxia (301171.SZ) announced its plan to issue H-shares for listing. This company, which also focuses on overseas AI marketing, achieved revenue of 2.717 billion yuan and net profit attributable to the parent company of approximately 204 million yuan in the first three quarters of 2025. Even earlier, in June 2024, BlueFocus (300058.SZ), which is also promoting AI transformation, submitted its listing application to the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. Its revenue in the first three quarters of 2025 reached 51 billion yuan, far exceeding Titanium Technology's size. Furthermore, Mininglamp Technology, dubbed the "world's first Agentic AI stock," successfully listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange in November 2024. This means that even though Titanium Technology has secured the title of "first Multi-Agent stock," it is not alone in the Hong Kong stock market. It will face a host of competitors with similar concepts but varying sizes, leading to increasingly fierce price wars and competition for customers. On the one hand, Titanium Technology's track record of continuous profitability, high gross margins, strong cash flow, and over 100,000 customers demonstrates that AI can indeed transcend the initial technology trial phase and achieve real commercial monetization in vertical marketing scenarios. It shows the market that when large models are combined with massive amounts of closed-loop business data, traditional workflows can indeed be restructured, creating quantifiable cost reduction and efficiency improvement value. On the other hand, over $500 million in accounts receivable, a heavy reliance on top media channels, a continuously declining gross profit margin, and competition from rivals like Yeahmobi and BlueFocus all remind investors that the true potential of this "AI marketing dark horse" still needs to be tested by time. For founder Li Shuhao, the journey from UC to Alibaba and then to entrepreneurship has taken nearly ten years, and the Hong Kong Stock Exchange bell has yet to ring; the real test has only just begun.

data.title: Meme Watchlist: Who's Cultivating Genuine Fans and Who's Manipulating Data?
data.description:
Source: The DeFi Report; Author: Michael Nadeau; Translation: BitpushNews

Whether you like it or not, we believe memecoins will exist for a long time. This article will share a macro perspective on the industry and delve into memecoins that we are closely monitoring.

Let's get started.

Why We're Focused on Memecoins

Our Methodology

Some people love memecoins, and some people hate them. We're trying to step outside this debate and simply observe the market. From our observation, memecoins have taken root. From a "product-market fit (PMF)" perspective, they seem to be the ultimate crypto "game." People love trading, they love the game, they love the social aspects, and they love the risk-reward dynamic.

Furthermore, we believe that participants are very clear about the game they are playing. After witnessing the success of PEPE in the Ethereum ecosystem, we seized upon BONK and Solana at the end of 2023. This led us to delve deeper into the space, including on-chain data and additional investments such as GIGA, PEPE, and SPX6900. Our goal is to uncover unique attributes in holder data that indicate the token's staying power and the holders' unwavering conviction. Why choose BONK, PEPE, SPX6900, PENGU, and USELESS? Liquidity: Each token boasts ample liquidity and is tradable on major exchanges and retail channels. Audience Scope: Each token has a global audience interest (rather than being limited to niche memes such as specific politics). Leaders/Embedded Culture: Each token is backed by at least one of the following: 1) a highly influential leader/holder group; 2) an established internet meme/culture; or 3) an existing popular brand. Historical Performance: Each token has experienced multiple significant price increases followed by sharp pullbacks. This price action demonstrates broad market interest and helps diversify the holder structure, laying the foundation for the next expansion. No Unlocking, No Venture Capital: Only transparent on-chain data. High Beta: Each token exhibits a high beta coefficient against BTC and its ecosystem's L1 assets. With proper configuration (our target is 3-5%), coin selection, and entry point, this can significantly improve risk-adjusted returns. Now, let's delve into the on-chain data to see if we can separate the wheat from the chaff. Total number of token holders: BONK: 985,900 current holders (currently at its peak). Launched in December 2022, BONK is the largest meme coin in the Solana ecosystem, deeply integrated into various applications and wallets. Note that BONK has conducted community-driven airdrops, "spreading" them to a large number of wallets, as shown in the table below. PENGU: 534,100 holders (down 14% from peak). Launched in December 2024, PENGU represents social interest in the popular brand Pudgy Penguin. The total market capitalization of the Pudgy Penguin NFT series is now approximately $78.5 million, while its meme coin market capitalization is $454 million (5.7 times that of the NFT series, down from its peak of $2.7 billion). We believe the value of the Pudgy Penguin brand (a strong crypto brand that has broken into the mainstream) is likely to continue flowing into this meme coin. PEPE: 505,700 current holders (down 1.51% from peak). PEPE launched in April 2023. We believe PEPE is the purest expression of internet meme culture in cryptocurrency, especially Ethereum. It's based on the globally recognized "Pepe the Frog," an image that has maintained a strong internet presence since the late 2010s. SPX6900: 113,000 current holders (down 0.33% from peak). Launched in September 2023, SPX6900 spawned a fervent internet community led by Murad, with the core idea of ​​"Flipping the stock market." The brand seems to combine financial satire with a "BTC-like" holder base that takes holders very seriously (as shown in the table below). We successfully traded SPX6900 during the last bull market and have been continuously analyzing its community and on-chain data since August 2024. USELESS: 38,600 current holders (down 5.2% from peak). Launched in May 2025 as a meme coin with a satirical "anti-utility" bias, USELESS attracted a vertical and seemingly loyal community after its listing on LetsBONK last year (at the time, it took market share from Pump Fun). With a current market capitalization of $44 million and daily liquidity exceeding $10 million (at a bear market low), we believe USELESS could present interesting opportunities under the right market conditions.

Holders with positions below $100

Conclusion:

BONK, PENGU, and PEPE already have a very mature holder base. SPX6900 exhibits a more steadfast and loyal holder base (albeit a smaller one).

... Holders with over $1,000 holdings. [Image of a PNG file: https://images.bitpush.news/cn/20260307/177283280691066654.png] Note: $1,000 wallets are categorized by the number of units held at the token's all-time high (ATH) equaling $1,000. Conclusion: SPX6900 stands out here, consistent with its performance since we started tracking this data. To better understand the retention rate of $1,000 holders, we normalized the all-time high price data for each token to calculate the number of token units required to become a "$1,000 holder" at that time. We then tracked changes in the holdings (number of units) of these wallets over time. This was done to filter out the noise of price fluctuations and gain a more accurate understanding of holders' retention intentions. Here are our findings: [Image of SPX6900] Conclusion: In terms of retention rate among "$1,000 holders" (measured by unit quantity to remove price impact), the SPX6900 appears to have outperformed its peers. Holders with over $100,000 holdings: We categorize $100,000 wallets by the number of units held at the token's all-time high (ATH) equaling $100,000. (Image of a wallet with over $100,000 holdings) Similar to the $1,000 holders data, we categorize $100,000 wallets by the number of units held at the ATH.

Then, we tracked these wallets to determine how the total number of “$100,000 wallets” (in token units) changed over time.

Here are our findings:

Conclusion:

This again demonstrates that SPX6900 leads in token holder retention at the $100,000 level.

...

Whale Retention Rate

Another way to monitor whale retention is to count all wallets that have ever held more than $100,000 worth of tokens, and then compare them with the number of wallets that still hold more than 50% of their peak holdings (in units). Here are our findings: Momentum and the 50-Week Moving Average (WMA) Relative Strength alt="image.png">

When we buy meme coins, we look for entry points where the 14-day RSI is in or near the oversold zone (30).

50-Week Moving Average

  • SPX6900: Trading at 64% below the 50-week moving average.

  • BONK: Trading at 62% below the 50-week moving average.

  • PENGU: Trading at 58% below the 50-week moving average.

  • PEPE: Trading at 57% below the 50-week moving average.

Drawdown percentage from all-time high

Conclusion

Given the industry's attention, we believe it's wise for crypto investors to pay attention to Meecoin. We find Meecoin to be highly valuable in monitoring broad risk appetite (Risk-on/off) and liquidity conditions in the crypto market.

Needless to say, with the right position size and entry point, the right assets can provide a powerful catalyst for a portfolio. We successfully captured BONK and SPX6900 in the last bull market (exiting with a very small portfolio position and achieving a return of over 10 times). We will be looking for similar entry opportunities in the future.

data.title: Special Report on Stablecoins and Non-Custodial Wallets: Risks and Countermeasures
data.description:
On March 3, 2026, the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) released its "Stablecoins and Non-Custodial Wallets: P2P Transactions" report. Based on case studies, industry research, and data analysis submitted by FATF global network members, the report delves into the risks of stablecoins in money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing. It reveals how different types of criminal organizations use stablecoins to transfer and launder funds, repeatedly emphasizing that the greatest risk comes from P2P (peer-to-peer) transactions without any regulated intermediaries. When funds flow between multiple self-custodial wallets, regulators and financial institutions have virtually no way to trace the true identities of the transacting parties. The FATF did not release any new mandatory standards this time, but rather reiterated that its existing recommendations (especially Recommendation 15) must apply to all participants in the stablecoin ecosystem, including issuers and intermediaries. This article by Beosin will interpret the core content of the report to help readers quickly grasp the risk and threat trends in the stablecoin field and improve their awareness and ability to respond to stablecoin-related risks.

I. Current Status of the Stablecoin Market

1. Explosive Growth in Scale

II. Existing Risks of Stablecoins

The report divides the stablecoin lifecycle into three stages: issuance, circulation, and redemption, and points out the risk points in each stage:

Because users have complete control over their private keys and do not rely on any regulated VASPs or financial institutions as intermediaries, peer-to-peer transactions are naturally outside the regulatory coverage of anti-money laundering obligations. Criminals are exploiting this characteristic by frequently creating new addresses through multi-layered transfers and then discarding them, thus fragmenting and dispersing funds. This makes it difficult for law enforcement to determine whether a peer-to-peer transaction is a normal transfer by an ordinary user or an illegal activity by a criminal network or sanctioned entity. This address proliferation tactic greatly increases the complexity of on-chain tracking. Public blockchain data alone is insufficient to form an effective chain of evidence; it is necessary to combine off-chain intelligence, address tag libraries, and advanced analytical tools to penetrate the anonymity layer.

The report further emphasizes that there is no legal obligation for parties involved in peer-to-peer transactions between non-custodial wallets to submit Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) to financial intelligence agencies.

While compliant VASPs enforce travel rules and monitor transactions when clients transfer funds to their non-custodial wallets, once funds leave the VASP's sight and hop between multiple non-custodial wallets, the VASP becomes unable to track them, allowing criminals to evade regulatory networks. This could be a structural loophole in anti-money laundering regulations in the virtual asset space. When funds are completely outside the purview of licensed institutions, traditional "person-based" regulatory models become ineffective. In the future, regulators may require VASPs to implement stricter due diligence measures for non-custodial wallet transactions and may even push for technological solutions (such as smart contracts with built-in blacklists and whitelists).

Original Content

2. Cross-Chain Transaction Activities

Compared to other virtual assets, blockchain networks and developers are prioritizing the development of cross-chain interoperability for stablecoins. This technology allows stablecoins to circulate freely between multiple blockchains (such as Ethereum, Solana, TRON, etc.) and different jurisdictions, achieving efficient cross-border transfers. However, cross-chain transactions increase traceability difficulty, weaken the ability of stablecoin issuers to implement control, and cause related stablecoin transactions to escape the regulated system.

Market analysis shows that sanctioned entities and other threat actors are increasingly using cross-chain activities to use stablecoins. They use "chain hopping" technology to split a transaction into multiple segments and repeatedly transfer them between different blockchain networks, thereby fragmenting the flow of funds and complicating the tracking path. Each blockchain operates independently and is inherently unable to interact with other blockchains.

Once funds are transferred via cross-chain bridges, tracking tools on the original chain become ineffective. Furthermore, cross-chain interoperability can weaken one of the most critical controls for stablecoin issuers – the ability to freeze or blacklist assets. The report specifically mentions that when centrally issued stablecoins are "wrapped" into new tokens on another chain via cross-chain bridges (e.g., crossing USDC from Ethereum to Solana, becoming "wormhole-wrapped USDC" on the Solana chain), these wrapped tokens often fall outside the direct control of the original issuer. Orders to freeze assets on the original chain may not be transmitted to the wrapped assets on other chains. Beosin believes stablecoin issuers should closely monitor the circulation of their tokens within the cross-chain ecosystem. Where possible, issuers should establish cooperation mechanisms with cross-chain bridge projects to ensure that freeze orders are transmitted to the wrapped assets as much as possible. VASP and compliance teams should also deploy analytics tools that support multi-chain tracking and cross-chain attribution, maintaining high vigilance regarding transactions involving cross-chain bridges and wrapped assets.

Original Content

3. Data Gap

The blockchain architecture of stablecoins plays both a supporting role and an obstacle to anti-money laundering/counter-terrorist financing regulation. Although all transactions are immutably recorded on a public blockchain, these records lack crucial off-chain information. This is why FATF Recommendations 10 and 15 (regarding customer due diligence) are so important: law enforcement agencies must be able to obtain customer identity information and geographical location from VASPs and financial institutions. Without this off-chain information, it is impossible to identify criminal suspects based solely on on-chain data. Furthermore, the lack of geographical location information of the underlying wallets severely weakens the ability of international cooperation between law enforcement agencies of different countries. When the flow of funds is unclear, and even the jurisdiction in which it occurred is unknown, information sharing and joint law enforcement are out of the question. Relying solely on publicly available data from blockchain explorers only reveals the flow of funds, not who is behind it or where they are located. This is precisely why criminals dare to act recklessly on public blockchains; they know that as long as cash isn't withdrawn through a KYC-compliant exchange, the on-chain address is a "safe haven." Secondly, not all transactions occur on-chain. When two users transfer funds within the same exchange, it's essentially an off-chain transaction. Asset changes are only recorded in the exchange's internal ledger and are not actually broadcast or confirmed on the blockchain. These transactions are often faster and cheaper (no miner fees), but at the cost of being completely outside the public blockchain's view. If the exchange is unlicensed or unregistered, the situation spirals out of control. No regulated intermediaries collect customer identification information, and no one submits suspicious transaction reports. Law enforcement agencies are completely unaware that the transaction occurred and have no way to investigate. This is a replica of the traditional financial "underground bank" problem in the virtual asset field. Off-chain transactions have created a regulatory "dark forest." While funds can be controlled when flowing within licensed institutions, they completely disappear from regulatory scrutiny once they enter the internal ledgers of unlicensed institutions. Criminals only need to deposit funds into an unlicensed exchange, let them circulate a few times on its internal ledger, and then withdraw them, completely cutting off on-chain tracing. In response to the data gaps revealed by the FATF, Beosin suggests increasing the crackdown on unlicensed and unregistered exchanges to cut off the channels for criminals to launder funds through off-chain transactions. For licensed institutions, customer due diligence information is not only a compliance requirement but also a firewall to protect themselves from being exploited by criminals. Institutions should also establish robust internal ledger monitoring systems to identify abnormal internal flow patterns.

Original Content

3. Summary of Recommendations

The report also lists several recommendations for various jurisdictions and the private sector to prevent the abuse of stablecoins, including:

● Apply FATF Recommendation 15 to the entire stablecoin ecosystem, define the AML/CFT responsibilities of issuers, VASPs, and other participants, and impose corresponding compliance requirements based on risks, such as requiring stablecoin holders to complete due diligence (whitelisting) in advance.

● Require issuers to possess the technical capability to execute the burning, freezing, and redemption of stablecoins in the secondary market; conduct due diligence on customers during the redemption phase; and restrict issuance activities on high-risk blockchains. Consider requiring issuers to implement whitelist and blacklist mechanisms; establish strict pre-issuance and pre-authorization supervision and compliance review mechanisms to prevent money laundering/terrorist financing risks related to stablecoins in advance. ● Strengthen the technical capabilities of regulatory agencies and law enforcement departments, cultivate their understanding of emerging risks, modus operandi, business models, smart contract functions, and cross-chain transaction mechanisms, and enhance their ability to effectively utilize blockchain analytics tools. This can be achieved through in-depth technical exchanges with blockchain analytics tool service providers and regular research into emerging criminal methods in the market. ● Provide regulatory agencies and law enforcement departments with the necessary tools for rapid cooperation with corresponding domestic and international institutions, including the rapid exchange of stablecoin-related information through established channels, MOUs, and legal provisions. ● Consider establishing a public-private partnership mechanism to strengthen cooperation among regulatory agencies, law enforcement agencies, and stakeholders in the stablecoin ecosystem regarding crime patterns, risk indicators, and emerging threats; establish cooperative relationships when necessary, especially in investigations involving off-chain transactions or stablecoin freezing/destruction. Conclusion The FATF report shows that the illegal abuse of stablecoins has become one of the focuses of global regulation, with peer-to-peer transactions in non-custodial wallets, cross-chain activities, and data gaps being the core risks and vulnerabilities. FATF recommends that all jurisdictions apply FATF Recommendation 15 to all participants in the stablecoin ecosystem, clarify the compliance obligations of issuers, VASPs, and other entities, strengthen the programmable control of smart contracts (such as blacklists and whitelists, freezing/destruction functions) and the application of blockchain analytics tools, and establish public-private partnership mechanisms to strengthen the regulation of stablecoins and balance the innovative value of stablecoins with financial security.

data.title: Is the end of the world for working people coming? The wave of layoffs in 2026 has only just begun.
data.description:
Author: Byron Gilliam; Translator: BitpushNews

Even when my former investment bank was doing well, I always felt another round of layoffs was imminent—I think part of the reason was that management simply didn't know how many people they actually needed.

I worked in the sales and trading floor, where at the end of each day there was a revenue figure: client commissions minus trading losses (occasionally, a profit). So you might think that quantifying who contributed what and who caused the losses should be a piece of cake.

But that's not the case.

The commission paid for a trade could be partly or entirely attributed to the research analyst, salesperson, or sales trader who spoke with the client—or to the trader on the other side of the trade (which was me back then!).

Nobody really knew why clients chose to trade with us.

Therefore, it's impossible to definitively attribute every commission to a specific individual, making it impossible to determine who is absolutely essential to the business. To paraphrase (department store magnate) Wanamaker, half of the payroll might be wasted; they just don't know which half. The only way to find out is to fire some people and see what happens. It feels like something similar is about to happen in companies everywhere, because it's not just investment banks facing this dilemma. When work was primarily concentrated in agriculture and manufacturing, measuring employee productivity was easy: simply count how many apples they picked or how many parts they produced. However, things become much more difficult when most people start working in offices. "Knowledge work is not defined by quantity," Peter Drucker wrote. "Knowledge work is not defined by its cost. Knowledge work is defined by its output." Employers don't know how to measure these outputs—what is the unit of output for a day's meetings, phone calls, and internal memos? So they turn to measuring time: employees are asked to spend eight hours a day in the office for pay, and employers expect them to complete eight hours of work within those eight hours. Time becomes a substitute metric for output. But what happens when everyone works from home? If employers can't measure employees by the time they spend in the office, they have to turn to measuring their output. This is a good thing. "Emphasizing output, rather than activity, is key to increasing productivity," Peter Drucker wrote in 1967. But employers have never really figured out how to do it. Now, artificial intelligence (AI) is forcing employers to try again. Large language models can handle many time-consuming tasks, so employers are starting to rethink what they're paying employees to do. I'm not sure they'll do a better job than the bank I used to work for. But the AI ​​narrative is putting enormous pressure on companies to find ways to improve productivity, to the point that many are simply laying off employees to see how things go. Data from March 6 suggests that this may have already begun: The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that tech jobs fell by 12,000 last month, and by a total of 57,000 over the past year. Strong productivity data was also released this week, which some economists see as the first sign that companies are beginning to use AI productively. So, companies may soon be able to do more with fewer people. But they may simply be doing more. A new paper in the Harvard Business Review found that "artificial intelligence doesn't reduce work; it only increases the intensity of work." In an eight-month study of work practices at a tech company, the authors found that AI led employees to work at a faster pace, take on a wider range of tasks, and work longer hours throughout the day. "Many people send AI prompts while eating lunch, in meetings, or waiting for documents to load. Some describe sending a 'last quick prompt' before leaving their desks so the AI ​​can continue working while they walk away." This sounds good to employers looking to extract more value from their employees. This part sounds even better: "Employees are increasingly absorbing tasks that previously might have required additional staff or staffing." But researchers warn employers: Seemingly higher productivity in the short term can mask silent workload proliferation and increasing cognitive stress as employees handle multiple AI-driven workflows simultaneously. Because the extra effort is voluntary and often described as a "fun experiment," leaders can easily overlook how much extra workload employees are actually taking on. Over time, overwork impairs judgment, increases the likelihood of errors, and makes it harder for organizations to distinguish between genuine productivity gains and unsustainable workloads. If so, companies may soon find they need more people, not fewer. At least, that's what IBM's HR executives anticipate. Nick Ramoroks told Bloomberg that cutting early-stage hiring might save money in the short term, but it could lead to a shortage of mid-level managers later on. Therefore, IBM plans to triple its entry-level hiring. "That's right," Ramoroks said, "it's for those jobs that everyone says AI can do." The investment bank I used to work for was constantly hiring between rounds of layoffs—constantly changing employees while trying to figure out who was doing what. The entire U.S. economy may soon follow suit. Let's look at the chart. This morning's jobs report was "brutal" for the tech industry. Losing 57,000 jobs in the past year is "almost as bad as the worst of the tech recession in 2024, and significantly worse than the recessions of 2008 or 2020." The tech industry is just the tip of the iceberg. Looking at the broader U.S. economy, according to a report by Challenger, Gray & Christmas, a global re-employment and executive coaching firm, employers announced 48,307 job cuts in February. This figure represents a 55% decrease from the 108,435 announced in January, and a significant 72% decrease from the 172,017 announced in the same month last year. In January and February of this year, the total number of layoff announcements reached 156,742, the fewest at the beginning of the year since 2022 (when only 34,309 were laid off in the first two months). However, this number still ranks fifth highest among all years since 2009. In other words, the wave of layoffs has indeed slowed compared to the beginning of the year and the same period last year, but it is still not low when viewed in a historical context. Life for working people won't get better so quickly. Too many leaders? An academic paper has found that generative AI is creating a "seniority-biased technological shift" in the employment field, with a particularly strong impact on junior employees. This isn't just happening in the tech industry: the study analyzed resume data from 285,000 employers. The same study explains that the decline in entry-level employment "was achieved entirely through a decline in hiring." The Artificial Intelligence Effect: Websites where people have long sought purchasing advice, such as Wired and Tom's Guide, have seen a sharp drop in traffic. We're now asking chatbots directly—and the source of their information is precisely those websites they've squeezed out of the market. Is it artificial intelligence?

Applied Artificial Intelligence Professor Alex Imas pointed out that this week's productivity data "shows signs" that companies are already benefiting from artificial intelligence.

Are people just talking?

Works that Artificial Intelligence Can and Cannot Do:

That's a great job, if you can get the job:

Annual salary: $405,000–$485,000.

These are some job openings at Anthropic and their salaries. Code is being written, but someone has to tell the code what to write, and this is a high-paying job.

Claude is winning:

An incredible chart from Ramp shows the contrast between OpenAI's shrinking share (blue) in the commercial market and Claude's growing share (orange).

Time Misalignment: A Gartner study predicts that "artificial intelligence will not bring 'job doomsday'—but job chaos." They expect that starting in 2028, AI will create more jobs than it eliminates. Call me a "doomsday optimist," I think it will all happen faster than we think. Have a great weekend, hardworking readers!

Vitalik Buterin posted on the X platform that he is proposing to replace the current finality component Casper FFG with the new Minimmit in the Ethereum consensus mechanism discussion. He believes that Casper FFG uses a two-round finality mechanism (validators need to sign twice to complete "proof" and "final confirmation"), while Minimmit only requires one round of signing to complete finality. As a trade-off, Minimmit's fault tolerance threshold will decrease from Casper FFG's 33% to approximately 17%. Although the fault tolerance ratio is reduced, Minimmit is more likely to lead the network into a state of "multi-chain competition rather than erroneous final confirmation" under various attack or client vulnerability scenarios, a situation that is easier to recover from through community coordination.

He believes that compared to the eventual confirmation of erroneous blocks, the absence of finality in the short term is safer and more recoverable, therefore he thinks Minimmit may be better in terms of overall security trade-offs.

Federal Reserve Governor Milan stated that the Fed typically doesn't react to oil prices. If there's any impact, it would only incentivize me to adopt a more dovish policy. An oil shock could drag down core inflation by harming demand.

▌International Energy Agency: Logistics Disruptions Pose Challenges, Global Oil Supply Remains Ample

International Energy Agency (IEA) Executive Director Fatih Birol stated that logistical disruptions caused by the conflict in the Middle East are posing challenges to many countries, but global oil supplies remain ample. Birol was attending an EU meeting on the global energy market situation in Brussels that day. When asked by the media whether the IEA was considering using its emergency oil reserves, Birol said, "All options are under discussion," but there are no plans to use them at this stage.

He said that we do not have an oil shortage problem; the current problem is a temporary disruption in logistics. (CCTV News)

Golden Encyclopedia

▌What is the value of BTC?

BTC is often compared to "digital gold," but its core value is actually the "promise of immutable scarcity." First, BTC has monetary properties, but it is not a currency because it lacks a stable unit of account. It is a pure "monetary property," that is, a consensus game among humans regarding "what can serve as a store of value." Gold's core value is also this, but gold's scarcity depends on the geological constraints of the physical world, while BTC's scarcity depends on mathematics and game theory; it is a scarcity at the information level.

But what makes BTC truly unique is not the coin itself, nor the "digital gold" narrative, but rather that blockchain is the first permissionless and unclosable value settlement layer in human history. This is a completely new category of infrastructure.

No sovereign entity can unilaterally prevent the final settlement of a BTC transaction. This property may be underestimated in peacetime, but it will be repriced during periods of geopolitical conflict and escalating capital controls. BTC is a systemic hedge. It is not a hedge against inflation, nor against the US dollar. It hedges against the structural degradation of institutional trust. In other words, BTC's value is not endogenous, but reflexive: the more people believe it has value, the more valuable it becomes, and vice versa. Therefore, BTC is a financialized expression of humanity's distrust of its own institutional capabilities. Its ceiling depends on the depth and breadth of this distrust, and its floor depends on the existence of a superior alternative.
