data.title: How capital reshapes asset prices in conflict
data.description:
Written by: Bitget Wallet

As gunfire reignites in the Middle East, colleagues in Dubai report bombings and air raid sirens; missiles pierce the sky, symbolizing humanity's uncertain fate.

Meanwhile, on another unseen timeline, global financial markets have begun to recalculate: Where will oil prices rise? Will gold continue its ascent? When will the stock market bottom out and rebound?

Capital shows no sympathy, nor does it rage. It simply does one thing calmly—pricing uncertainty. For most people, it is invisible, incomprehensible, its logic cold, its pace ruthless.

But in turbulent times, understanding the logic of capital operations and risk pricing may be the last line of defense between ordinary people and the torrent of history.

Looking back at the history of geopolitical conflicts and finance, you'll find a remarkably consistent pattern: in the face of war, capital markets always repeat the same script, and this script has been fully played out four times in the past 36 years. What capital fears most is not conflict, but "waiting." From the Gulf War in 1991, the Iraq War in 2003, to the Russia-Ukraine conflict in 2022, the script is always identical. These three globally influential geopolitical crises demonstrate the pricing pattern of capital markets in the "incubation period—outbreak period—clarity period." Financial markets are essentially a discounting machine for expectations. When a conflict is in its incubation period, the fear of unknown supply disruptions pushes oil and gold prices to record highs, and global stock markets plummet. However, Wall Street has a bloody iron rule: "Buy to the sound of cannons." Once the first shot is fired (or the situation becomes clear), the greatest uncertainty is cleared up. Safe-haven assets often peak and fall quickly, while the stock market completes a deep V-shaped reversal from the depths of despair. The war may continue, but the panic in capital markets has ended. The following is an in-depth analysis of the changes in capital markets during these three historical events: 1. 1990-1991 Gulf War: A Classic "V-Shaped Reversal" and the Oil Price Shock This war is a textbook case study in modern financial history for studying geopolitical shocks, perfectly illustrating the principle of "buy the expectation, sell the fact."

  • The Crisis Brewing Period (August 1990 – January 1991): Panic and Risk Aversion

    • Soaring Oil Prices: Following Iraq's invasion of Kuwait, the market was gripped by extreme panic, fearing a disruption in Middle Eastern oil supplies. Within just two months, international oil prices surged from around $20 per barrel to over $40, an increase of more than 100%.

    • Stock Market Crash: Affected by soaring oil prices and the looming threat of war, the S&P 500 index in the United States plummeted by nearly 20% between July and October 1990.

    • The shoe finally dropped (January 17, 1991): A Counterintuitive Market Shift. On the first day of Operation Desert Storm, led by the US military, the market experienced an extremely counterintuitive trend: as the war progressed overwhelmingly, "uncertainty" vanished instantly. Oil prices plummeted: on the day the war began, oil prices experienced one of the largest single-day drops in history (plummeting by over 30%). Stock market euphoria: the S&P 500 surged that day, subsequently initiating a sharp V-shaped reversal, not only recovering all losses within six months but also reaching a new all-time high. 2. The 2003 Iraq War: A "Relief" After a Prolonged Decline The 2003 Iraq War, compounded by the lingering effects of the bursting dot-com bubble and post-9/11 security anxieties, led to a market reaction that reflected a "better short, sharp pain than a long, drawn-out one" relief. The crisis's incubation period (late 2002 – March 2003): A Slow and Steep Pain During the months-long diplomatic tug-of-war and war preparations, capital markets were extremely volatile. The S&P 500 continued its downward spiral, with global capital flowing into gold and US Treasury bonds due to risk aversion. Crude oil prices slowly rose from $25 to nearly $40 due to factors such as war expectations and strikes in Venezuela. The dust settled (March 20, 2003): The worst was over. Ironically, the absolute bottom for US stocks occurred a week before the war began (around March 11, 2003). When missiles actually landed on Baghdad, the market viewed it as the worst being over. The stock market subsequently surged, initiating a four-year bull market. Safe-haven assets such as gold cooled rapidly after the war progressed smoothly.

3. 2022 Russia-Ukraine Conflict: "Super Stagflation" Triggered by Supply Chain Disruption

Unlike the previous two Middle East wars (where the US achieved a swift and overwhelming victory without causing long-term substantial damage to the global supply chain), the Russia-Ukraine conflict had a more profound and severe impact on capital markets, and altered the underlying logic of the macroeconomy.

  • Crisis Outbreak (February 2022): An Epic Commodity Storm

    • Russia is a global energy and industrial metals giant, while Ukraine is the "breadbasket of Europe." After the outbreak of the conflict, Brent crude oil once broke through $130 per barrel; European natural gas prices soared several times; and commodity prices such as wheat and nickel reached record highs.

Russia is a global energy and industrial metals giant, while Ukraine is the "breadbasket of Europe." After the outbreak of the conflict, Brent crude oil once broke through $130 per barrel; European natural gas prices soared several times; and commodity prices such as wheat and nickel reached record highs.

  • Lasting Impact: The "Double Kill" of Inflation Rebound and Monetary Tightening

    • Stock and Bond Markets Plunge: The most devastating market impact of the Russia-Ukraine conflict is that it completely shattered the fragile global supply chains after the pandemic, directly triggering the most severe inflation in Europe and the United States in 40 years.

    • To combat this "imported inflation" caused by geopolitical conflict, the Federal Reserve was forced to initiate the most aggressive interest rate hike cycle in history. This resulted in a rare "double kill" in stocks and bonds in 2022 (stocks fell, and bonds also fell), with the Nasdaq index plunging by more than 30% that year.

    A Deadly Illusion: Never Try to Profit from War

    Let's rewind to reality.

    The sudden escalation of tensions in the Middle East has once again plunged global capital markets into a period of uncertainty and "stress testing."

    From the perspective of the macroeconomic transmission chain, the core threat of the Middle East conflict to capital markets lies in "physical supply chain disruption → soaring energy prices → global inflation rebound → central banks forced to maintain tightening → a plunge in risky assets."

    Analysis of the Chain Reaction in the Capital Markets

    1. International Crude Oil: The Absolute Center of the Storm

    Chain Reaction: The Middle East controls the lifeline of global crude oil (especially key shipping routes such as the Strait of Hormuz). If the conflict escalates or risks affecting major oil-producing countries, the market will immediately factor in a "geopolitical risk premium." This will lead to a short-term surge in Brent and WTI crude oil prices.

    Deep Impact: Crude oil is the mother of all industries. Soaring oil prices will not only increase costs in the aviation, logistics, and chemical industries, but will also directly threaten the recently stabilized global Consumer Price Index (CPI) in the form of "imported inflation."

    Precious Metals (Gold/Silver): The Traditional Ultimate Safe Haven Chain Reaction: In the face of war, geopolitical instability, and potential hyperinflation, funds instinctively flow into gold. Gold prices typically gap up before and in the early stages of a conflict, reaching new highs, even historical highs; silver, due to its industrial properties, exhibits greater volatility than gold. Deeper Impact: It's important to note that gold price surges are often driven by sentiment. Once the situation becomes clearer (even if the conflict continues), safe-haven demand subsides, and gold prices are highly likely to experience a pullback, returning to a pricing logic dominated by the real interest rate of the US dollar.

    1. US Stock Market: The Specter of Inflation and "Valuation Kill"

    Chain Reaction: War is generally negative for US stocks. The VIX (Volatility Index) will surge rapidly, and funds will withdraw from overvalued tech stocks (such as AI and semiconductors) and flow into defensive sectors such as defense, traditional energy, and utilities.

    Deeper Impact: What US stocks fear most is not the fighting in the Middle East, but the inflationary rebound triggered by the fighting. If soaring oil prices lead to persistently high US CPI, the Federal Reserve will be forced to postpone interest rate cuts or even raise rates again. This tightening of macro liquidity will severely impact the valuation of tech stocks, represented by the Nasdaq.

    1. Crypto Market: Liquidity Drain on High-Risk Assets

    Chain Reaction: Despite Bitcoin's long-standing narrative as "digital gold," in past genuine geopolitical crises (such as the initial outbreak of the Russia-Ukraine conflict and the escalation of tensions in the Middle East), the crypto market's actual performance has been more like a "highly volatile Nasdaq index."

    Deeper Impact: Faced with war panic, Wall Street institutions prioritize selling the most liquid and riskiest assets to cash out, with the crypto market often bearing the brunt and experiencing a downturn. Simultaneously, altcoins face liquidity depletion. However, when conflicts trigger the collapse of fiat currencies in certain regions, or when traditional banking systems are disrupted, the "censorship-resistant and borderless" safe-haven attributes of crypto assets will be favored by some safe-haven funds.

    By comparing three historical geopolitical conflicts, we can extract the core principles for ordinary people to cope with geopolitical crises: "Uncertainty" is the biggest killer: The most severe stock market declines almost always occur during the preparatory and strategic phases before the outbreak of war. Once war actually begins (especially when the situation becomes predictable), the stock market often bottoms out and rebounds. This confirms the Wall Street adage: "Buy when the guns are roaring." The "buying trap" in commodities: Before and in the early stages of war, crude oil and gold are often pushed to incredibly high prices due to panic. However, if the war does not substantially and permanently cut off physical supplies (as in the Gulf and Iraq wars), prices will quickly halve after the war begins. Blindly chasing high commodity prices easily makes one a bagholder for institutional investors. Distinguishing between "emotional shocks" and "fundamental damage": If a war is merely an emotional shock (such as a localized conflict with a significant power imbalance), the stock market will quickly recover after a fall. However, if a war leads to a long-term disruption of core supply chains (such as the energy/food crisis triggered by the Russia-Ukraine conflict), it will alter the global pricing anchor of funds through "inflation and interest rate hikes," in which case the market's adjustment period will be very long. History doesn't simply repeat itself, but it often rhymes. When observing current capital anomalies, we need to calmly assess whether the current conflict is merely a temporary emotional panic or a black swan event that will truly reshape the global inflation and interest rate cycle. Geopolitical games are unpredictable; a ceasefire declaration late at night can instantly wipe out highly leveraged positions bought at high prices. In a crisis, the primary principle is always to preserve capital. Defensive Strategy in Turbulent Times: How Should Ordinary People Make Their Moves? Under the dual shadows of war and inflation, the core objective of ordinary investors must shift from "pursuing high returns" to "preserving principal, defending against inflation, and hedging tail risks." It is recommended to reorganize assets according to the following "defensive counterattack" strategy: Strategy 1: Build a Strong Cash Moat (20%-30%) Method: Increase holdings of cash and cash equivalents (such as high-interest dollar deposits, short-term treasury bonds, and money market funds). Logic: In times of crisis, liquidity is the lifeline. Having sufficient cash on hand not only ensures your family's quality of life isn't affected by soaring prices in extreme circumstances, but also gives you the funds to "buy the dip" in quality assets after a market crash. Strategy 2: Buy Inflation "Insurance" (10%-15%) Method: Allocate a suitable amount to gold ETFs, physical gold, or a small amount to broad-based energy ETFs. Logic: The purpose of this portion of funds is not to make huge profits, but to hedge. If war leads to oil supply disruptions and soaring prices, the increased cost of living can be offset by the rise in gold and energy stocks. Remember: Don't buy in at the peak when headlines are everywhere. Strategy 3: Shrink the battle lines and defend core equities (30%-40%). Method: Sell highly leveraged, unprofitable marginal stocks and concentrate funds on broad-based index ETFs (such as the S&P 500) or large companies with strong cash flow. Logic: During wartime, individual stocks face extremely high black swan risks (such as sudden supply chain disruptions leading to bankruptcy). Embracing broad-based indices uses the systemic resilience of the nation and the entire economy to hedge against the vulnerability of individual companies. As long as you persist with regular investment and ignore short-term losses, crises often create long-term "golden opportunities." Strategy 4: "De-risking" of Crypto Assets (for Web3 Users) Methods: Appropriately reduce holdings of high-volatility altcoins and Meme coins; consolidate funds into Bitcoin (BTC) as a long-term core holding, or convert them into USD stablecoins (USDC/USDT) and deposit them into a leading compliant platform to earn flexible interest. Once geopolitical risks are deemed manageable and market liquidity returns, you can allocate 10-30% of your funds to invest in Meme coins, depending on your risk appetite, to seize alpha opportunities. Logic: Liquidity crises caused by war have a greater impact on small-cap cryptocurrencies. Stablecoins can both hedge against risk and provide more flexible liquidity reserves than traditional banks during crises.

    The absolute red line that must not be crossed

    1. Strictly prohibit leverage: Geopolitics is constantly changing; a ceasefire declaration can cause oil prices to plummet by 10%. In leveraged trading, you may not see long-term gains and will be wiped out in a short-term shock.

    2. Abandon the gambling mentality of "profiting from war": Information asymmetry in the capital market is extremely cruel. When you decide to go long on a certain asset class because you see the war escalating, Wall Street quantitative institutions are often already prepared to "take profits and sell the fact."

    In the face of massive macroeconomic shocks, the most powerful weapon for ordinary people is not accurate prediction, but common sense, patience, and a healthy balance sheet.

    The flames of war will eventually be extinguished, and order will always be rebuilt on the ruins.

    At the height of extreme panic, the most counterintuitive action is to remain rational, and the most dangerous move is to panic and liquidate all holdings. Remember the oldest adage in the investment world: never gamble on the end of the world—because even if you win, no one will pay you back. Ultimately, our greatest wish remains for the conflict to subside, for families separated to reunite, and for world peace.

data.title: When L1 zkEVM becomes the endgame, when will the next revolution arrive?
data.description:
Written by: imToken

From a purely perceptual perspective, the Ethereum core developer community has been updating at an unusually high frequency since 2025.

From the Fusaka upgrade to Glamsterdam, and then to long-term plans for the next three years focusing on kEVM, quantum-resistant cryptography, and gas limits, Ethereum has released multiple roadmap documents covering three to five years in just a few months.

This pace itself is a signal.

If you carefully read the latest roadmap, you will find a clearer and more aggressive direction emerging: Ethereum is transforming itself into a verifiable computer, and the end point of this path is L1 zkEVM.

...

I. Three Shifts in the Ethereum Narrative

On February 26th, Ethereum Foundation researcher Justin Drake posted on social media that the Ethereum Foundation had proposed a draft roadmap called Strawmap, outlining the upgrade direction of the Ethereum L1 protocol over the next few years.

This roadmap proposes five core goals: faster L1 (second-level final confirmation), "Gigagas" L1 achieving 10,000 TPS through zkEVM, high-throughput L2 based on Data Availability Sampling (DAS), quantum-resistant cryptography, and native privacy transfer functionality; the roadmap also plans for seven protocol forks by 2029, averaging about one every six months.

It can be said that, over the past decade, Ethereum's development has been accompanied by a continuous evolution of its narrative and technological roadmap. The first phase (2015–2020) was the programmable ledger. This was the core of Ethereum's initial narrative: "Turing-complete smart contracts." At that time, Ethereum's biggest advantage lay in its ability to do more than Bitcoin. For example, DeFi, NFTs, and DAOs were all products of this narrative. Numerous decentralized finance protocols began running on the chain, from lending and DEXs to stablecoins, and Ethereum gradually became the main clearing network for the crypto economy. The second phase (2021–2023) saw the narrative takeover by L2. As Ethereum mainnet gas fees soared, making transaction costs unaffordable for ordinary users, Rollups began to take center stage in scaling. Ethereum also gradually repositioned itself as the settlement layer, aiming to provide a secure foundation for L2. Simply put, this involved migrating most of the computation from the execution layer to L2, scaling through Rollups, while L1 was only responsible for data availability and final settlement. During this period, The Merge and EIP-4844 served this narrative, aiming to make L2 use Ethereum's trust more cheaply and securely. The third phase (2024–2025) focused on the involution and reflection within the narrative. As is well known, the prosperity of L2 has brought an unexpected problem: Ethereum L1 itself has become less important. Users are starting to operate more on Arbitrum, Base, and Optimism, and rarely directly interact with L1. The price performance of Ethereum (ETH) also reflects this anxiety. This has led to debate within the community: if L2 has captured all the users and activity, where will the value of L1 be captured? Until the internal turmoil of Ethereum in 2025, and the latest series of roadmaps rolled out in 2026, this logic is undergoing a profound evolution. In fact, reviewing the core technological directions since 2025 reveals a recurring theme: Verkle Trees, Stateless Clients, EVM formal verification, and native ZK support. These technologies all point to the same goal: making Ethereum Level 1 (L1) verifiable. It's important to note that this doesn't just mean making L2 proofs verifiable on L1, but rather ensuring that every state transition in L1 can be compressed and verified using zero-knowledge proofs. This is precisely the ambition of L1 zkEVM. Unlike L2 zkEVM, L1 zkEVM (in-shell zkEVM) means directly integrating zero-knowledge proof technology into the Ethereum consensus layer. It's not a replica of L2 zkEVM (like zkSync, Starknet, Scroll), but rather a transformation of Ethereum's execution layer itself into a ZK-friendly system. So, if L2 zkEVM is building a ZK world on top of Ethereum, then L1 zkEVM is turning Ethereum itself into that ZK world. Once this goal is achieved, Ethereum's narrative will upgrade from an L2 settlement layer to "verifiable computational root trust." This will be a qualitative change, not the quantitative change of the past few years. II. What is the true L1 zkEVM?

As always, in the traditional model, validators need to "re-execute" every transaction to verify a block. However, in the zkEVM model, validators only need to verify one ZK Proof. This allows Ethereum to increase its Gas Limit to 100 million or even higher without increasing the burden on nodes (further reading: "ZK Route 'Dawn': Is Ethereum's Endgame Roadmap Accelerating?").

However, transforming Ethereum L1 into zkEVM is not a single-point breakthrough issue; it requires simultaneous progress in eight directions, each a multi-year project.

**Workline 1: EVM Formalization** All ZK proofs presuppose that the object being proven has a precise mathematical definition. However, the behavior of today's EVM is defined by client implementations (Geth, Nethermind, etc.), not by a rigorous formal specification. Different clients may behave inconsistently in boundary cases, making it extremely difficult to write ZK circuits for the EVM. After all, you can't write a proof for a vaguely defined system. Therefore, the goal of this workline is to write every instruction and every state transition rule of the EVM into a machine-verifiable formal specification. This is the foundation of the entire L1 zkEVM project. Without it, everything that follows is like building a tower on sand. **Workline Two: Replacement with ZK-Friendly Hash Functions** Ethereum currently uses Keccak-256 as its hash function. Keccak is extremely unfriendly to ZK circuits, incurring enormous computational overhead and significantly increasing the time and cost of proof generation. The core task of this workline is to gradually replace Ethereum's internal Keccak usage with ZK-friendly hash functions (such as the Poseidon and Blake series), especially in state trees and Merkle proof paths. This is a far-reaching change, as hash functions permeate every corner of the Ethereum protocol. **Workline Three: Replacing Merkle Patricia Tree with Verkle Tree** This is one of the most anticipated changes in the 2025–2027 roadmap. Ethereum currently uses Merkle Patricia Tree (MPT) to store the global state. Verkle Trees replace hash links with Vector Commitments, which can compress the witness size by tens of times. For L1 zkEVM, this means a significant reduction in the amount of data required to prove each block, a significant increase in proof generation speed, and that the introduction of Verkle Trees is a key infrastructure prerequisite for the feasibility of L1 zkEVM. **Workline Four: Stateless Clients** Stateless clients refer to nodes that, when verifying blocks, do not need to store the complete Ethereum state database locally; they only need the witness data attached to the block itself to complete the verification. This workline is deeply tied to the Verkle Tree because stateless clients are only practically feasible if the witness is small enough. Therefore, the significance of stateless clients for L1 zkEVM is twofold: firstly, it significantly lowers the hardware barrier for running nodes, contributing to decentralization; secondly, it provides clear input boundaries for ZK proofs, allowing provers to process only the data contained in the witness, rather than the entire world state. Workline Five: Standardization and Integration of ZK Proof Systems L1 zkEVM requires a mature ZK proof system to generate proofs for block execution. However, the current ZK technology landscape is highly fragmented, with no universally accepted optimal solution. The goal of this workline is to define a standardized proof interface at the Ethereum protocol layer, allowing different proof systems to access the platform competitively, rather than being assigned to a single system. This maintains the openness of the technology and leaves room for the continuous evolution of the proof system. The Ethereum Foundation's PSE (Privacy and Scaling Explorations) team has already accumulated a great deal of preliminary experience in this direction. Workline Six: Decoupling the Execution Layer and Consensus Layer (Engine API Evolution) Currently, Ethereum's Execution Layer (EL) and Consensus Layer (CL) communicate through the Engine API. Under the L1 zkEVM architecture, each state transition of the Execution Layer requires the generation of a ZK proof, and the generation time of this proof may far exceed the block interval. The core problem that this workline needs to solve is how to decouple execution and proof generation without breaking the consensus mechanism—execution can be completed quickly first, proof can be generated asynchronously with a delay, and then the validator can complete the final confirmation at an appropriate time. This involves a deep overhaul of the block finality model. Workline Seven: Recursive Proofs and Proof Aggregation Generating a single ZK proof for a single block is very costly, but if proofs from multiple blocks can be recursively aggregated into a single proof, the verification cost will be significantly reduced. The progress of this workline will directly determine how low a cost L1 zkEVM can operate. Workline Eight: Developer Toolchain and EVM Compatibility Guarantee All underlying technical modifications must ultimately be transparent to smart contract developers on Ethereum. The existing hundreds of thousands of contracts cannot become invalid due to the introduction of zkEVM, and developers' toolchains cannot be forced to be rewritten. This workline is the most easily underestimated, but often the most time-consuming. Every EVM upgrade in history has required extensive backward compatibility testing and toolchain adaptation. The changes to L1 zkEVM are far greater than previous upgrades, and the workload for toolchain and compatibility will increase by orders of magnitude. III. Why is now the right time to understand this? The release of the Strawmap coincided with market doubts about ETH's price performance. From this perspective, the most important value of this roadmap lies in redefining Ethereum as "infrastructure." For developers and other builders, Strawmap provides directional certainty; for users, these technological upgrades will ultimately translate into a tangible experience: transactions are confirmed within seconds, assets flow seamlessly between L1 and L2, and privacy protection becomes a built-in feature rather than a plugin. Objectively speaking, L1 zkEVM is not a product that will be available anytime soon; its full implementation may not be until 2028-2029 or even later. But at least it redefines Ethereum's value proposition. If L1 zkEVM succeeds, Ethereum will no longer be just an L2 settlement layer, but a verifiable root of trust for the entire Web3 world, allowing any on-chain state to ultimately be mathematically traceable back to Ethereum's ZK proof chain. This is crucial for Ethereum's long-term value capture. Secondly, it also affects the long-term positioning of L2. After all, when L1 itself possesses ZooKeeper capabilities, the role of L2 will change—evolving from a "secure scaling solution" to a "dedicated execution environment." Which L2 systems can find their place in this new landscape will be the most noteworthy ecological evolution to observe in the coming years. Most importantly, I believe it is also an excellent window into Ethereum's developer culture—the ability to simultaneously advance eight interdependent technical worklines, each a multi-year project, while maintaining decentralized coordination, is itself a unique capability of Ethereum as a protocol. Understanding this helps to more accurately assess Ethereum's true position in various competing narratives. In summary, from the "Rollup-centric" approach in 2020 to the Strawmap in 2026, Ethereum's narrative evolution reflects a clear trajectory: scaling cannot rely solely on L2; L1 and L2 must evolve collaboratively. Therefore, the eight working threads of L1 zkEVM are a technological reflection of this shift in understanding. They all point to a single goal: to achieve orders-of-magnitude performance improvements on the Ethereum mainnet without sacrificing decentralization. This is not a negation of the L2 approach, but rather its refinement and supplement. Over the next three years, this "Ship of Theseus" will undergo seven forks and countless "planks" replacements. When it reaches its next destination in 2029, we may see a truly meaningful "global settlement layer"—fast, secure, private, and as always, open. Let's wait and see.

data.title: Kraken pushed open the door to the American financial system.
data.description:
In the US financial system, there is a channel that almost all institutions must go through.

Whether it's bank transfers, large-value clearing, or Treasury bond transaction settlements, they all ultimately enter the same system—the Federal Reserve Payment Network.

And the number of institutions that can directly enter this network is not large. The vast majority of financial institutions can only connect to the Federal Reserve through banks as an "intermediary layer."

But now, this boundary has been broken for the first time. On March 4th, Kraken Financial, a Wyoming-based chartered bank under the cryptocurrency exchange Kraken, received approval for its master account from the Federal Reserve, becoming the first digital asset bank in U.S. history to directly access the Federal Reserve's payment system. This means that, for the first time, dollar settlements can be completed directly between the "crypto institution and the Federal Reserve." Many industry insiders believe this is not only a technological advancement, but also a significant milestone in the US encryption regulatory system. What is Fedwire?

Fedwire is a real-time large-value payment network operated by the Federal Reserve System, mainly used for:

  • Government bond transaction clearing

  • Liquidity transfer between financial institutions

SPDI has two key features:

1. 100% reserve

 Entering the Core of Finance 

Once exchanges can directly access Fedwire, several important changes will occur:

1. Institutional Funding Efficiency Significantly Improved The intermediate steps are significantly reduced.

2. Decreasing Dependence on Banks

In the past few years, the crypto industry has experienced so-called "bank cutoffs" multiple times:

  • Settlement restricted

  • Funding channel cut off

Asset Custody

  • Institutional Fund Management

  • Cross-Market Liquidity Hub

    • The situation has turned around

    If the past decade has seen the crypto industry trying tointegrate into the traditional financial system. What's happening now seems to be taking a different turn: Traditional financial infrastructure is opening doors to the crypto industry. Kraken's acquisition of the Federal Reserve's master account may just be the beginning of this shift. As more and more crypto institutions enter the core financial system, a new question arises: Will the future dollar-based financial system be dominated by banks, or will it be jointly constituted by new digital financial institutions? This game surrounding financial infrastructure may have only just begun.

data.title: After OpenClaw's explosive popularity: Which US stocks were affected by this open-source crayfish?
data.description:
In November 2025, an Austrian independent developer, Peter Steinberger, quietly submitted a project to GitHub – Clawdbot (now renamed OpenClaw). At the time, no one paid attention, but everything spiraled out of control by the end of January 2026. Between January 29th and 30th, the project gained tens of thousands of GitHub stars in a very short time, quickly surpassing 100,000. As of March 3rd, this number had ballooned to nearly 250,000, topping the star charts and surpassing Linux. For reference, star open-source projects like React (one of the world's most popular front-end development frameworks) and Linux (the operating system kernel that supports internet servers) often take more than ten years to accumulate 200,000 stars, while OpenClaw's curve is almost a vertical line.

OpenClaw was originally named Clawdbot, a name that sounds like Claude. On January 27th, Anthropic sent a lawyer's letter forcing them to change the name. The project was transferred to Moltbot and finally settled on the name OpenClaw. However, the name change did not slow its spread; instead, it generated more buzz. On February 16th, Sam Altman announced that Steinberger had joined OpenAI, and OpenClaw would be transferred to an independent open-source foundation supported by OpenAI.

OpenClaw was originally named Clawdbot, a name that sounds like Claude. On January 27th, Anthropic sent a lawyer's letter forcing them to change the name. The project was transferred to Moltbot and finally settled on the name OpenClaw.

Token Killer App: The Super Flywheel for Large Model Service Providers

If Agents become the mainstream paradigm for AI interaction, the API revenue of large model providers will experience exponential growth.

However, the two largest Agent model providers, OpenAI and Anthropic, are not yet publicly listed. Therefore, the most direct IPOs corresponding to this logic in the capital market are MSFT and Google.

First, as OpenAI's largest external shareholder, every API request made by Microsoft through Azure OpenAI Service to call GPT-4o or o1 essentially contributes revenue to Microsoft's cloud business. The fact that the founders of OpenClaw joined OpenAI and transferred the project to the OpenAI-supported foundation means that the OpenClaw ecosystem will likely be more closely tied to OpenAI models in the future. If OpenAI becomes the top-ranked default model in OpenClaw's recommended list in the future, Microsoft will essentially gain access to a developer portal with 240,000 GitHub stars. Alphabet, on the other hand, benefits from a different dimension: the publicly traded company to which Google belongs (stock code GOOGL / GOOG). Google's Gemini series is one of the mainstream models supported by OpenClaw, and Gemini 2.0 Flash boasts highly competitive inference cost-effectiveness. More importantly, among the leading model vendors, Alphabet is one of the few AI model providers that can be directly invested in through the secondary market. Even more noteworthy is that the market currently doesn't seem to have fully priced in the agent-driven API consumption logic. GOOGL hasn't seen a significant increase since February due to OpenClaw, while MSFT has experienced a valuation correction. In other words, the expectation gap still exists, meaning the capital market is still valuing model companies using the logic of "chatbots," rather than the continuously operating Agent economy. If token consumption is the gasoline of the Agent era, then GPUs are the engine driving this machine, and the most direct beneficiaries are still GPU manufacturers NVIDIA and AMD. Over the past three years, the market's valuation logic for chip companies has primarily been based on the training side, with major manufacturers vying to purchase GPUs to train increasingly larger basic models. However, training is more of a phased investment, while inference is a continuous consumption; for example, every tool call by each agent constantly triggers new inference requests. As agents move from the laboratory to millions of users, the proportion of demand on the inference side is expected to increase significantly. This also explains NVIDIA's new narrative. If the large-scale single-order growth on the training side slows down, what can sustain GPU demand? The Agent's answer is the continued increase in inference volume. NVIDIA's latest financial report shows that Q4 2026 revenue increased by 73% year-on-year, indicating strong demand. The rise of the Agent paradigm provides a more sustainable underlying explanation for this strength. Let's look at AMD. On February 4th, AMD's stock price plummeted 17% due to its Q1 financial report falling short of expectations, triggering widespread market panic. However, just 20 days later, Meta announced a $60 billion (5-year) AI chip supply agreement with AMD, along with warrants for up to 160 million shares, approximately 10%, which seems more like a deep strategic partnership. Why does Meta need so much inference computing power? Because it's pursuing so-called personal superintelligence, and realizing this vision requires a massive number of agents running continuously in the background. OpenClaw is validating not just a product direction, but the entire logic of the agent's need for massive computing power. Therefore, the growth in inference demand driven by agents will first be transmitted to the computing power layer, with NVDA and AMD being the core targets. Among companies that continuously consume computing power at the application layer, Meta may also become an important driver of demand. The True Carrier of Agent Scalability: Cloud Computing As mentioned earlier, GPUs are the engine of the Agent era; cloud computing platforms are the infrastructure for the long-term operation of these agents. From a capital market perspective, the core targets in this chain are the three major cloud platforms AMZN, MSFT, and GOOGL. Further upstream in the data center infrastructure layer, EQIX and DLR may also be indirect beneficiaries. While OpenClaw touts local deployment, the reality is that due to security and permission issues, most users won't run an AI Agent on their laptops 24/7. Whether for individuals or enterprises, the ultimate goal of large-scale deployment is likely cloud deployment. Alibaba Cloud and Tencent Cloud have already launched one-click deployment services in the Chinese market, which indirectly confirms the genuine demand. Furthermore, there's an easily overlooked detail: the value of agents to the cloud isn't just computing power, but also long-tail inference traffic. AI training orders are characterized by "large clients + large orders + periodicity," while agent inference is driven by "a large number of small clients + high-frequency calls + continuous revenue"—a business model that cloud vendors prefer. Globally, the three major cloud vendors each possess unique advantages. AWS, as the world's largest cloud platform, supports multiple model APIs on its Bedrock platform, making it a common deployment environment for developers. Azure benefits from both model APIs and cloud infrastructure, with Azure OpenAI Service's exclusive GPT access capabilities further amplified in agent scenarios. Google Cloud's differentiation lies in its cost structure. The inference price of models like Gemini Flash is significantly lower than that of many flagship models. In scenarios requiring long-term Agent operation and token consumption, this price difference will be rapidly amplified. Another point worth noting is that if Agents operate at scale, the computing power demands of cloud providers will ultimately translate into data center construction, potentially indirectly benefiting Equinix and Digital Realty. The popularity of OpenClaw validates a trend: people are willing to let AI do the work for them, not just chat with them. However, for the traditional enterprise software sector, this is seen by the market as the prelude to the "SaaSpocalypse" (the end of SaaS). At the start of 2026, SaaS giants collectively faced pressure: Salesforce fell 21% year-to-date, and ServiceNow fell 19%. The root of this panic lies in a structural game between agents and software. In the past, to command a system, we needed a software interface; now, agents can directly invoke the system to complete tasks, and the software's own presence is being diminished. This change brings two fundamental problems. First, the impact of AI is not limited to the "per-user" model, but affects the entire software value chain. For example, Adobe's stock price plummeted from a high of $699.54 to $264.04, a drop of 62%; education software company Chegg crashed from $115.21 to $0.44, almost to zero; and tax software giant Intuit also plunged 16% in a week in January 2026. The market's concern isn't about a particular pricing model being disrupted, but rather that generative AI tools (such as Anthropic) are automating core enterprise workflows, reducing reliance on traditional software functions, thus permanently compressing the revenue potential of the entire SaaS platform. Secondly, the more powerful the agent, the more vulnerable the traditional business model becomes. For example, Microsoft is eroding ServiceNow's pricing power and slowing down new customer acquisition through its "Agent 365" bundling strategy. A simple deduction is enough to send chills down investors' spines: if one AI agent can do the work of 100 employees, is it still necessary for a company to buy 100 software seats? OpenClaw's mainstream success is essentially accelerating the realization of this logic. Of course, the giants haven't sat idly by. Salesforce's AgentForce has achieved $800 million in ARR, a year-on-year increase of 169%; ServiceNow's Now Assist annual contract value has exceeded $600 million, and is expected to reach $1 billion by the end of the year. But it's never easy for giants to dance; they're caught in the classic innovator's dilemma: new agent revenue is growing, but old seat revenue is shrinking, and the outcome of this two-pronged race remains unclear. For CRM and NOW, the core contradiction is – can the incremental growth of agents fill the gap in the seat model? The market has already voted with its feet to provide the answer. Meanwhile, Palantir tells a completely different story. This company focuses on helping governments and large enterprises make critical decisions using AI: the military uses it to analyze battlefield intelligence, and businesses use it to optimize supply chains, predict risks, and deploy AI in the most complex and sensitive business scenarios. After a brief pullback in February, PLTR quickly rebounded, stabilizing around $153 in early March. While the SaaS sector was battered by the "SaaS doomsday" narrative, Palantir bucked the trend and strengthened. This divergence may mean that the winners of the Agent era may not be the fastest-transforming old giants, but rather companies born for AI from the outset.
